coredata.dc:title: Optimization of ultrasonic-assisted electrodeposition for high-performance CQDs-PPy/NPG composite electrodes in supercapacitors

coredata.dc:description: In this study, carbon quantum dots-polypyrrole/nanoporous gold (CQDs-PPy/NPG) composite electrodes were developed using ultrasonic-assisted electrodeposition with ultrasonic power levels ranging from 50 W to 200 W. The effects of ultrasonic power on the structural and electrochemical performance of the electrodes were systematically investigated. The electrode prepared at 150 W achieved a specific capacitance of 673.6F/g at a scan rate of 10 mV/s and 627.1F/g at 100 mV/s, reflecting a rate capability of 93.1 %. In contrast, the electrode prepared at 50 W demonstrated a specific capacitance of 437.8F/g at 10 mV/s and 251.0F/g at 100 mV/s, underscoring the considerable improvement in capacitance with higher ultrasonic power. Galvanostatic charge–discharge (GCD) tests at a current density of 5 A/g showed that the 150 W sample achieved a charging time of 408 s and a discharging time of 395 s, corresponding to a coulombic efficiency of 96.8 %. This composite electrode also exhibited excellent cycling stability, retaining 94.2 % of its initial capacitance after 20,000 cycles. Furthermore, it demonstrated an energy density of 513.7 Wh/kg at a power density of 5 W/kg. These findings suggest that optimizing ultrasonic power, particularly at 150 W, significantly improved the dispersion of CQDs and PPy, resulting in enhanced electrochemical performance. This composite emerges as a promising candidate for advanced supercapacitor applications. Furthermore, the CQDs-PPy/NPG composite was integrated into an asymmetric supercapacitor (ASC) using activated carbon (AC) as the negative electrode, achieving a high energy density of 42.8 Wh/kg at a power density of 2000 W/kg.

head.ce:title: Optimization of ultrasonic-assisted electrodeposition for high-performance CQDs-PPy/NPG composite electrodes in supercapacitors

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, carbon quantum dots-polypyrrole/nanoporous gold (CQDs-PPy/NPG) composite electrodes were developed using ultrasonic-assisted electrodeposition with ultrasonic power levels ranging from 50 W to 200 W. The effects of ultrasonic power on the structural and electrochemical performance of the electrodes were systematically investigated. The electrode prepared at 150 W achieved a specific capacitance of 673.6F/g at a scan rate of 10 mV/s and 627.1F/g at 100 mV/s, reflecting a rate capability of 93.1 %. In contrast, the electrode prepared at 50 W demonstrated a specific capacitance of 437.8F/g at 10 mV/s and 251.0F/g at 100 mV/s, underscoring the considerable improvement in capacitance with higher ultrasonic power. Galvanostatic charge–discharge (GCD) tests at a current density of 5 A/g showed that the 150 W sample achieved a charging time of 408 s and a discharging time of 395 s, corresponding to a coulombic efficiency of 96.8 %. This composite electrode also exhibited excellent cycling stability, retaining 94.2 % of its initial capacitance after 20,000 cycles. Furthermore, it demonstrated an energy density of 513.7 Wh/kg at a power density of 5 W/kg. These findings suggest that optimizing ultrasonic power, particularly at 150 W, significantly improved the dispersion of CQDs and PPy, resulting in enhanced electrochemical performance. This composite emerges as a promising candidate for advanced supercapacitor applications. Furthermore, the CQDs-PPy/NPG composite was integrated into an asymmetric supercapacitor (ASC) using activated carbon (AC) as the negative electrode, achieving a high energy density of 42.8 Wh/kg at a power density of 2000 W/kg.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In the current global context, energy demand is rapidly increasing due to continuous industrial growth and an expanding global population [1–3]. However, relying on fossil fuels to meet this demand has raised significant environmental concerns. The combustion of these fuels is a major contributor to greenhouse gas emissions, driving climate change and resulting in severe ecological impacts [4,5]. Further, the finite nature of fossil fuel reserves raises concerns about the sustainability of this energy source. These challenges highlight the need for developing environmentally friendly and clean energy conversion systems that provide higher energy conversion efficiency while minimizing adverse environmental impacts [6,7]. Addressing these issues is essential for achieving a sustainable energy future, where renewable energy sources and advanced energy storage systems play a crucial role.

Supercapacitors (SCs), also known as ultracapacitors, are a promising class of energy storage devices that bridge the gap between traditional capacitors and batteries [8]. The working principle of SCs is based on the electrostatic storage of energy at the interface between the electrode material and the electrolyte. When a voltage is applied, ions from the electrolyte accumulate on the electrode's surface, forming an electric double layer. This mechanism, known as electrochemical double-layer capacitance, enables SCs to store and release energy rapidly [9,10]. Furthermore, some SCs operate through a pseudocapacitance mechanism, where rapid and reversible redox reactions take place at the surface of the electrode material, further enhancing energy storage capacity [11]. Compared to conventional capacitors, which have limited energy storage capacity and lower energy densities, supercapacitors offer significantly higher energy densities, faster charge–discharge cycles, and a longer lifespan [12]. These advantages make SCs an attractive option for various applications, including portable electronics, electric vehicles, and grid energy storage systems [13]. The performance of SCs significantly depends on the properties of the electrode materials used. Nanoporous gold (NPG), with its unique porous structure and high surface area, offers excellent electrical conductivity, mechanical stability, and a large active surface area for charge storage. These characteristics enhance the overall energy storage capacity and efficiency of SCs, making NPG an effective material for advanced electrode applications [14,15]. Li et al. [16] investigated the in-situ electrochemical behavior of macroscopically nanoporous gold/polypyrrole (NPG/PPy) under compressive deformation. The results showed that the electrochemical capacitance of NPG/PPy decreased with increasing scanning rate and discharge current density during operation. Xu et al. [17] investigated the electrochemical and mechanical properties of PPy by integrating it into a pleated nanoporous ribbon (NPG@MG) composite electrode material. They found that the capacitance of the NPG@MG-PPy composite electrode reached 393 mF·cm−2, demonstrating excellent cycling capability after 8,000 charge/discharge cycles due to its mechanical flexibility. Aghazadeh et al. [18] prepared porous graphene (PG)/NPG/PPy composites through catalytic chemical vapor deposition (CCVD) and hydrothermal synthesis to investigate their surface morphology and electrochemical properties. The results showed that the specific capacity of the electrode material was as high as 374.5 mAh·g−1 at a current load of 0.5 A·g−1 and 277 mAh·g−1 at 10 A·g−1. PPy, a conductive polymer, has garnered significant attention in the field of electrochemical energy storage due to its unique properties. PPy, as an electrode material, presents numerous advantages, including exceptional electrical conductivity, significant environmental stability, and simple synthesis [19]. These attributes make PPy a promising candidate for significantly enhancing the performance of SCs. Despite its many advantages, PPy has certain limitations. One primary challenge is its relatively lower conductivity compared to other conductive polymers and carbon-based materials [20]. This limitation can result in reduced charge transfer efficiency, subsequently impacting the overall energy storage capacity and power density of SCs. Furthermore, PPy is susceptible to volumetric changes during the doping and developing processes, which may lead to mechanical degradation and a loss of capacitance over extended cycling [21]. To overcome these challenges, incorporating carbon quantum dots (CQDs) into PPy-based electrode materials has been proposed as an effective solution.

CQDs are a class of nanomaterials recognized for their exceptionally small size, typically under 10 nm, along with unique properties such as strong photoluminescence, excellent biocompatibility, and outstanding electrical conductivity [22,23]. In the context of SCs, CQDs provide several advantages that render them an ideal component for improving the performance of electrode materials [24]. Primarily, CQDs feature a high surface area that offers numerous sites for charge storage. This expansive surface area, coupled with their outstanding electrical conductivity, facilitates rapid charge and discharge processes, enhancing the overall power density of SCs [25]. Moreover, CQDs are recognized for their significant chemical stability and ease of functionalization, facilitating the development of composite materials with customized properties for targeted applications [26]. When incorporated into a conductive polymer such as PPy, CQDs can markedly improve the conductivity of the composite material. Moreover, CQDs serve as vital active sites for electrochemical reactions. Their surfaces are abundant in functional groups, including hydroxyl, carboxyl, and carbonyl groups, which actively participate in redox reactions. These functional groups not only enhance the pseudocapacitance of the electrode material but also facilitate stronger interactions between the CQDs and the electrolyte, resulting in improved charge storage and transfer capabilities [27,28].

Currently, multiple methods are used for preparing electrode materials for supercapacitors. Among the most prevalent techniques are chemical vapor deposition (CVD), sol–gel processing, hydrothermal synthesis, and template-assisted methods [29]. Arya et al. [30] developed porous carbon nanotube (CNT) structures coated with vanadium oxide particles using a controlled CVD process. The deposition resulted in a 17-fold increase in surface capacitance and a 36-fold increase in volume capacitance. Das et al. [31] employed a sol–gel synthesis method to prepare CuBO2 nanostructures, which were thoroughly characterized. The nanoparticles produced through this method exhibited pseudocapacitive properties, while the electric double-layer capacitance (EDLC) varied with the different cubic and rod-like nanostructures observed in the samples. Chavhan et al. [32] utilized template-assisted methods to create porous NiO and Co3O4 membranes on nickel foam. The thin-film electrodes produced through this approach exhibited hollow spherical and honeycomb structures, contributing to specific capacities of 108 mC·cm−2 (270 mF·cm−2) and 98 mC·cm−2 (245 mF·cm−2) at a current density of 1 mA·cm−2, respectively. While these techniques have been effective in preparing various electrode materials, they often involve complex procedures, elevated temperatures, or the use of hazardous chemicals. Furthermore, some methods fail to provide adequate control over the morphology and uniformity of the resulting materials at the nanoscale. Electrodeposition presents a highly attractive alternative for preparing electrode materials, especially for SCs [33]. This technique involves the electrochemical reduction of metal ions or other precursors onto a substrate, resulting in the formation of a thin film or coating [34]. One of the primary advantages of electrodeposition is its simplicity and cost-effectiveness [35]. Incorporating ultrasonic waves into the electrodeposition process significantly enhances its advantages. Ultrasonic-assisted electrodeposition (UAED) applies high-frequency ultrasonic waves during electrodeposition, forming cavitation in the electrolyte solution. This process leads to the formation and collapse of microbubbles, which promotes improved mass transport. Thus, UAED ensures a more uniform distribution of ions in the electrolyte and reduces concentration gradients near the electrode surface, resulting in superior material characteristics [36,37].

In summary, this study used ultrasonic electrodeposition to fabricate NPG electrodes coated with CQDs and PPy. This study is significant as it explores the synergistic effects of CQDs and PPy on NPG electrodes through UAED. The technique enhances material deposition uniformity and improves mass transport, leading to improved electrochemical performance. Incorporating CQDs addresses the limitations of conventional electrode materials like PPy by enhancing charge transfer efficiency and overall energy storage capacity. While many studies have investigated CQDs and PPy individually, the influence of ultrasonic power in preparing CQD-PPy/NPG composite electrode materials remains unexplored, representing a gap in the existing literature. This study explores the effect of ultrasonic power (50 W to 200 W) on the fabrication of CQD-PPy/NPG electrode materials. The innovative use of ultrasonic-assisted electrodeposition allows for improved control over the morphology and dispersion of CQDs in the PPy/NPG matrix, significantly enhancing the electrochemical performance of the composite electrode. Advanced characterization techniques, including Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM) and Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM), were employed to analyze the surface morphology of the experimental samples. The crystal structure and valence composition were examined using Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR), X-ray Diffraction (XRD), and X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS). Similarly, the electrochemical performance of the electrode materials was assessed through various tests, such as Cyclic Voltammetry (CV), Galvanostatic Charge-Discharge (GCD), and Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS), to ensure their reliability and practicality.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preliminary preparation of CQDs-PPy/NPG electrode material
ce:section.ce:para: The NPG electrode was fabricated using a silver-gold alloy thin film composed of Ag65Au35 (at.%). These films were sourced from Materion Corporation (United States) and provided as 100 nm-thick sheets, which were precisely cut into 2 cm × 2 cm pieces. The choice of Ag65Au35 was based on its proven effectiveness in producing nanoporous structures through dealloying, serving as the foundational substrate for subsequent material deposition. NPG was fabricated via a chemical dealloying process that selectively removes silver (Ag) from the Ag65Au35 alloy, resulting in a high-surface-area gold structure suitable for subsequent electrodeposition. The process involved immersing the Ag65Au35 thin films in a 70 % nitric acid (HNO3) solution at room temperature for 24 h, during which Ag was etched away to form the porous gold structure. Afterward, the NPG samples were rinsed thoroughly with deionized water and dried in a vacuum oven at 60 °C for 12 h. Details of the specific experimental reagents used are provided in Table 1 .

CQDs were synthesized via a hydrothermal method using sodium citrate (Na3C6H5O7·2H2O, Sigma-Aldrich, Germany) and ammonium bicarbonate (NH4HCO3, Alfa Aesar, United Kingdom) as precursors. A solution was prepared by dissolving 2 g of sodium citrate and 1 g of ammonium bicarbonate in 50 mL of deionized water. This mixture was transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave, which was sealed and heated to 180 °C for 12 h to facilitate carbonization and form CQDs. After cooling to room temperature, the solution was filtered through a 0.22 µm membrane to remove large particulates, followed by dialysis for 48 h to eliminate residual impurities. The synthesized CQDs exhibited high surface area and excellent electrical conductivity, making them well-suited for enhancing the performance of NPG-based electrode materials. This initial preparation provided a basis for further modifications of the NPG electrode through the incorporation of PPy and CQDs using ultrasonic electrodeposition.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation process of CQDs-PPy/NPG electrode material
ce:section.ce:para: Polyimide (PI) was selected as the substrate material for its higher thermal stability, mechanical strength, and exceptional flexibility, making it highly suitable for high-temperature processes [38]. The PI film was cut into 2 cm × 2 cm pieces to match the dimensions of the prepared NPG films. Before NPG deposition, the PI films were thoroughly cleaned to eliminate surface contaminants by sequentially sonicating them in acetone, ethanol, and deionized water for 10 min each, followed by drying with a nitrogen stream. The NPG films were then transferred onto the PI substrate using a physical pressing technique. To enhance adhesion between the NPG film and the PI substrate, as well as to improve the mechanical properties of the composite electrode material, an annealing process was conducted. The PI-supported NPG films were placed in a furnace and heated to 300 °C for 2 h in an argon atmosphere. This controlled annealing process ensured uniform bonding between the NPG and PI while preserving the nanoporous structure of the gold film, which is essential for its electrochemical performance.

The electrodeposition of PPy and CQDs onto the NPG/PI substrate was performed using UAED. A three-electrode electrochemical setup was used, with the NPG/PI composite as the working electrode, platinum (Pt) foil as the counter electrode, and a saturated calomel electrode (SCE) as the reference electrode. The electrolyte solution for electrodeposition was prepared by dissolving 0.1 M pyrrole monomer and the pre-prepared CQD solution (described in Section 2.1) in 100 mL of deionized water. To ensure uniform dispersion of the CQDs in the electrolyte, 0.1 M sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS) was added as a surfactant. Ultrasonic power ranging from 50 W to 200 W was applied during the electrodeposition process using an ultrasonic bath to investigate its effects on the preparation of CQDs-PPy/NPG electrode materials. The instruments used in the experiment are listed in Table 2 . Electrodeposition was conducted at a constant potential of 0.8 V vs. SCE for 30 min at room temperature. Ultrasonic agitation during deposition facilitated a uniform and nanostructured PPy-CQDs coating on the NPG/PI substrate. Following electrodeposition, the CQDs-PPy/NPG electrodes were rinsed with deionized water and dried under a nitrogen stream to remove residual electrolyte or unreacted materials. A schematic diagram of the electrodeposition process is shown in Fig. 1 Furthermore, the mass loading of the active material on the electrodes was carefully controlled and measured. The average active mass of the CQDs-PPy/NPG composite on the electrode surface was approximately 5 mg cm−2. Proper active material loading is essential for accurately evaluating the electrochemical performance of the electrode and for comparing results with other studies or commercial products [39].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication process of CQDs-PPy/NPG//AC asymmetric supercapacitor
ce:section.ce:para: The CQDs-PPy/NPG//AC asymmetric supercapacitor (ASC) was constructed using CQDs-PPy/NPG as the positive electrode and activated carbon (AC) as the negative electrode. The CQDs-PPy/NPG electrode was prepared by ultrasonic-assisted electrodeposition, as previously described, and dried in a vacuum at 60 °C for 12 h. For the AC electrode, activated carbon powder was mixed with polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) binder in a mass ratio of 85:10:5, with N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP) as the solvent. This mixture was coated onto carbon cloth and dried in a vacuum oven at 80 °C for 12 h. This slurry was then coated onto carbon cloth and dried in a vacuum oven at 80 °C for 12 h. The ASC was assembled by placing a polypropylene (PP) membrane separator between the CQDs-PPy/NPG positive electrode and the AC negative electrode, with the entire assembly immersed in a 1 M Na2SO4 aqueous electrolyte.

The electrochemical performance was evaluated through the following tests: first, cyclic voltammetry (CV) curves of both the AC and CQDs-PPy/NPG electrodes were measured at a 50 mV/s scan rate to assess individual performance. Next, CV measurements of the assembled CQDs-PPy/NPG//AC ASC were conducted at 50 mV/s across varying potential windows to observe overall electrochemical behavior. Finally, galvanostatic charge–discharge (GCD) tests were performed at current densities ranging from 1 A/g to 10 A/g, providing insights into the energy density and power density of the ASC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structure and performance characterization of CQDs-PPy/NPG electrode material
ce:section.ce:para:
The structural and morphological properties of the CQDs-PPy/NPG electrode material were characterized through SEM, TEM, XRD, XPS, and FTIR. SEM imaging, using a FEI Quanta 250 (USA) at 20 kV, provided detailed surface structure insights at varying magnifications. TEM, conducted on a JEOL JEM-2100 (Japan) at 200 kV, offered high-resolution images of the CQD nanostructures, their interaction within the PPy matrix, and the nanoporous structure of NPG. The samples were prepared by dispersing small amounts of the material on carbon-coated copper grids. X-ray diffraction (XRD) was performed using a Rigaku SmartLab X-ray diffractometer (Rigaku, Japan) equipped with a Cu Kα radiation source (λ = 1.5406 Å). Scanning was conducted over a 2θ range of 10° to 80° at a rate of 5°/min to analyze the crystalline structure of the CQDs-PPy/NPG electrode material. The resulting XRD patterns provided insights into the crystalline phases present in the electrode material, utilizing Bragg's law, as shown in Eq. (1) [40]:
(1)
2 d s i n θ = n λ
where d is the distance between the planes in the atomic lattice (cm), θ is the angle of incidence (°), n is an integer, and λ is the wavelength of the X-rays (m).

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was conducted using a Thermo Scientific ESCALAB 250Xi (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) with an Al Kα X-ray source (1486.6 eV) to analyze the elemental composition and chemical states of the CQDs, PPy, and NPG. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was performed on a Bruker Vertex 70 spectrometer (Bruker, Germany) over a range of 4000 cm−1 to 400 cm−1 to identify functional groups and bonding characteristics within the composite material.

The electrochemical performance of the CQDs-PPy/NPG electrode was evaluated using a CHI 660E Electrochemical Workstation (CH Instruments, USA). CV was conducted in a three-electrode system, with the CQDs-PPy/NPG electrode as the working electrode, a platinum wire as the counter electrode, and an SCE as the reference electrode, using 1 M KOH as the electrolyte. CV scans were performed at scan rates of 10 mV/s to 200 mV/s across a potential window of 0 V to 0.6 V vs. SCE. GCD measurements were carried out at various current densities (1, 2, 5, and 10 A/g) to assess specific capacitance and charge–discharge behavior. The specific capacitance was calculated from the area under the CV curve, using the following Eq. (2) [41]:
(2)
C
s p
=
I
m · ν
where I represent the current (A), m is the mass of the active material (g), and v is the scan rate (V/s).

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was conducted over a frequency range of 0.01 Hz to 100 kHz with a 5-mV amplitude to evaluate the internal resistance and charge transfer characteristics of the CQDs-PPy/NPG electrode. The data were fitted to an equivalent circuit model to determine the charge transfer resistance and related parameters.

The surface morphologies of the CQDs-PPy/NPG electrode materials fabricated at various ultrasonic powers (50 W, 100 W, 150 W, and 200 W) were analyzed using scanning electron microscopy (SEM). The SEM images in Fig. 2 illustrate the effects of ultrasonic power on the surface morphology. At 50 W (Fig. 2a), the surface exhibited a highly irregular texture with prominent clusters of CQDs-PPy material, indicating uneven distribution and a lack of homogeneity. As the ultrasonic power increased to 100 W (Fig. 2b), there was a slight improvement in uniformity; however, distinct agglomerations remained, and particle distribution was still inconsistent across the surface. At 150 W, as shown in Fig. 2c, the morphology exhibited a significant improvement. The surface became much smoother, featuring a continuous and dense film of CQDs-PPy material uniformly distributed across the NPG substrate. Minimal agglomeration was observed, and the nanostructure was refined. Zoomed-in views of the 150 W sample, illustrated in Fig. 2e and 2f, further revealed a highly compact and well-integrated layer of CQDs-PPy particles with excellent coverage and adhesion to the NPG structure. However, at 200 W, as depicted in Fig. 2d, while the deposition remained uniform, some evidence of overgrowth was noted, resulting in slightly less-defined particles compared to the optimized structure observed at 150 W. The variation in surface morphology is closely related to the ultrasonic power applied during the electrodeposition process. At lower powers, such as 50 W (Fig. 2a) and 100 W (Fig. 2b), the weak cavitation effect results in insufficient mass transport within the electrolyte [42], leading to uneven particle distribution, larger clusters, and inadequate coverage of the NPG substrate. As the power increases to 150 W (Fig. 2c), the intensified cavitation promotes efficient mixing of the CQDs-PPy particles, resulting in uniform deposition with minimal agglomeration and a finely structured nanomaterial. The zoomed-in views at this power (Fig. 2e and 2f) highlight the optimal integration of CQDs and PPy into the nanoporous gold matrix, producing a compact and homogeneous surface. At 200 W (Fig. 2d), while the enhanced cavitation effect improves mass transport, it also leads to less control over particle size and morphology. Therefore, 150 W represents the optimal ultrasonic power, achieving the best balance between uniformity, particle size control, and surface characteristics, ultimately enhancing electrode performance. Tudela et al. [43] assessed the influence of ultrasonic power on nickel layer preparation, determining that optimal crystal orientation was achieved at a power level of 0.124 W/cm3.

ce:section.ce:section-title: EDS element distribution of CQDs-PPy/NPG electrode materials
ce:section.ce:para: EDS analysis of the CQDs-PPy/NPG electrode material at 150 W ultrasonic power illustrated the spatial distribution of essential elements, such as carbon (C), nitrogen (N), oxygen (O), and gold (Au). The selected SEM area (Fig. 3 a) was examined to confirm the element uniformity across the surface. In Fig. 3b, carbon shows a highly uniform distribution, indicating effective integration of CQDs into the PPy matrix and even dispersion over the NPG surface. This uniform distribution of carbon is crucial for maintaining the electrode's conductivity and enhancing overall electrochemical performance by providing a consistent conductive network throughout the material [44]. Fig. 3c displays the nitrogen distribution, which is homogeneous across the selected area. Nitrogen, a crucial component of PPy, plays a significant role in the polymer's conjugated structure. Its even distribution suggests a uniformly deposited PPy layer on the NPG surface, promoting consistent electrochemical properties across the electrode. Similarly, Fig. 3d reveals a uniform oxygen distribution, indicating the presence of oxygen-containing functional groups within both the CQDs and the PPy matrix. These functional groups contribute to the pseudocapacitance effect, thus enhancing the overall capacitance of the electrode material [45]. Fig. 3e illustrates the gold distribution, corresponding to the underlying NPG structure. The even distribution of gold confirms that the CQDs-PPy layer was uniformly deposited over the NPG substrate. This uniformity ensures that the nanoporous gold structure is fully utilized as a conductive framework, therefore enhancing the electrode's conductivity and stability.

The TEM image in Fig. 4 a shows the CQDs dispersed on the sample surface, with a nearly spherical shape and a relatively uniform size distribution. The well-dispersed CQDs, without large aggregates, suggest effective synthesis and precise control of particle size. Liu et al. [46] treated mica powder surfaces during electroless copper plating using ultrasonic waves, successfully controlling the particle size to approximately 100 nm. These small CQDs offer a high surface area, which enhances electrochemical performance in SCs. Fig. 4b displays the size distribution of the CQDs, revealing an average size of 5.28 ± 0.39 nm. The small size of these particles is essential for improving electron transfer and providing a large active surface area, contributing to the high specific capacitance and rapid charge–discharge characteristics of the CQDs-PPy/NPG electrode materials. The TEM image of the CQDs-PPy/NPG electrode material in Fig. 4c demonstrates the successful integration of CQDs within the PPy matrix and NPG framework. This image highlights the close interaction between CQDs and the polymer matrix, which promotes strong electrical connectivity and contributes to efficient charge transport. This integration also enhances the high surface area crucial for optimizing supercapacitor performance. The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image of the CQDs-PPy/NPG material in Fig. 4d reveals lattice fringes for both the CQDs and NPG. The CQDs exhibited a lattice spacing of 0.226 nm, corresponding to the (100) plane of graphitic carbon, while the gold (Au) lattice planes show a spacing of 0.187 nm, associated with the (220) plane of gold. This confirms the crystalline structure of the CQDs and the conductive properties imparted by the gold framework, which are essential for optimizing the electrochemical performance of the electrode. The selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern in Fig. 4e exhibits distinct diffraction rings, indicating the polycrystalline nature of the CQDs-PPy/NPG composite. These rings correspond to the crystalline planes of both CQDs and NPG, specifically the (100), (220), and (111) planes. This diffraction pattern supports the findings from the HRTEM image, confirming the crystallinity and effective structural integration of CQDs within the NPG matrix.

The XRD patterns for the CQDs-PPy/NPG samples synthesized at varying ultrasonic powers (50 W, 100 W, 150 W, and 200 W) are presented in Fig. 5 . Characteristic diffraction peaks corresponding to the face-centered cubic (fcc) structure of gold (Au) are observed at approximately 2θ values of 38.2° (111), 44.4° (200), 64.6° (220), and 77.6° (311). These peaks are evident in all samples, confirming the presence of gold from the NPG substrate. A broad peak around 24-26° corresponds to the (100) plane of graphitic carbon in CQDs. This peak is more pronounced in samples prepared at higher ultrasonic powers (100 W, 150 W, and 200 W), indicating enhanced incorporation and dispersion of CQDs at these power levels. The broad nature of the CQDs peak suggests the presence of small, nanoscale CQDs with low crystallinity, which is characteristic of CQDs.

The XRD pattern of the CQDs-PPy/NPG sample synthesized at 50 W exhibited relatively low-intensity peaks for both CQDs and Au, indicating that lower ultrasonic power may lead to inadequate dispersion of CQDs and less efficient integration into the NPG structure. As the ultrasonic power increased to 100 W, the intensity of the CQDs peak around 24-26° became more pronounced, suggesting improved incorporation of the CQDs into the composite material. Furthermore, the gold peaks became sharper, indicating enhanced crystallinity of the gold phase. The sample synthesized at 150 W displayed the most prominent CQDs peak, indicating the highest degree of incorporation and dispersion of CQDs. The gold peaks remained sharp, and the overall pattern suggested an optimal balance between CQD distribution and gold crystallinity at this power level. In the 200 W sample, the CQDs peak intensity decreased slightly compared to the 150 W sample, while the gold peaks became broader. This observation suggests that at very high ultrasonic powers, there may be a slight overgrowth of the deposited material, leading to reduced uniformity in particle size and potentially impacting the crystallinity of the gold phase [47].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fourier transform infrared spectrometer analysis of CQDs-PPy/NPG
ce:section.ce:para: The FTIR spectra presented in Fig. 6 offer insights into the functional groups in both pure PPy/NPG and CQDs-PPy/NPG (synthesized at 150 W). Comparing these spectra reveals the impact of CQD incorporation on the composite's chemical structure. The broad absorption band at 3545 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibrations of O–H or N–H groups, likely originating from the CQDs [48]. The incorporation of CQDs significantly increased the presence of hydrophilic functional groups, enhancing wettability and improving electrolyte interaction, which is advantageous for supercapacitor applications. The peak at 1736 cm−1 corresponds to C = O stretching vibrations typically associated with carbonyl groups found in CQDs. Its presence in the CQDs-PPy/NPG sample indicates thesuccessful integration of CQDs, leading to additional oxygen-containing functional groups that can enhance pseudocapacitance through faradaic reactions. The peak at 1548 cm−1, characteristic of C = C stretching vibrations within the pyrrole rings of PPy, confirms the retention of the PPy structure after CQD incorporation. The strong intensity of this peak suggests that the conjugated backbone of PPy remains well-preserved, contributing to the material's overall conductivity. The peak at 1447 cm−1 corresponds to C-N stretching vibrations in PPy, signifying the presence of nitrogen in the polymer backbone. Its detection in both spectra indicates that the fundamental structure of PPy is preserved in both the pure PPy/NPG and CQDs-PPy/NPG samples. The anticipated N–H stretching peak of PPy in the 3300–3500 cm−1 range was not distinctly observed in the pure PPy/NPG spectrum. This may be due to several factors. One possibility is the overlap of N–H stretching vibrations with O–H stretching vibrations from adsorbed moisture or other surface-bound groups, which could obscure the distinct N–H peak. Moreover, the intensity of the N–H stretching vibration in PPy can vary depending on the degree of hydrogen bonding within the polymer matrix or with surrounding molecules [49]. In this instance, the hydrogen bonding environment in the PPy/NPG composite may have changed, diminishing the visibility of the N–H stretching vibration. Such an effect could arise from the interaction between PPy and the NPG substrate, potentially disrupting the usual hydrogen bonding interactions. The peak at 1259 cm−1 in the CQDs-PPy/NPG sample corresponds to C-O stretching vibrations, further confirming the integration of CQDs and the introduction of oxygen-containing functional groups. The peak at 1021 cm−1 is attributed to C–H in-plane bending vibrations, indicating that both samples retained the characteristic features of PPy. Further, the peak at 960 cm−1, which is more prominent in the pure PPy/NPG sample, is associated with the bending vibration of the N–H group in PPy. The reduced intensity of this peak in the CQDs-PPy/NPG sample indicates that the incorporation of CQDs may alter the hydrogen bonding environment within the PPy matrix, potentially affecting the visibility of the N–H stretching peak [50].

The XPS spectrum presented in Fig. 7 a confirmed the presence of key elements in the CQDs-PPy/NPG composite, highlighting three prominent peaks: C 1 s (284.8 eV), N 1 s (400 eV), and O 1 s (532 eV). These peaks indicate the successful incorporation of carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen species primarily derived from the CQDs and the PPy matrix [51]. Carbon arises from both the CQDs and the PPy backbone, while nitrogen is primarily associated with the PPy. Oxygen is introduced through functional groups in the CQDs, which are crucial for enhancing the material's pseudocapacitive behavior. In addition to the primary elements, the XPS spectrum displays several smaller peaks that may be attributed to trace impurities or elements adsorbed from the environment during synthesis or sample preparation. For example, minor peaks near 200 eV may correspond to chlorine, likely originating from residual precursors or solvents used in the fabrication of the composite. Similarly, adsorbed species from the electrolyte or atmosphere, such as adventitious carbon or minor contaminants, can also contribute to these peaks. These trace elements are often unavoidable due to the sensitivity of XPS and the complex interactions between the sample and its environment. However, these additional peaks do not significantly affect the primary characteristics of the CQDs-PPy/NPG composite, which are dominated by carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen.

In the high-resolution O 1 s spectrum (Fig. 7b), three distinct peaks were identified: O(I) at 530.2 eV, O(II) at 531.5 eV, and O(III) at 532.6 eV. The O(I) peak corresponds to oxygen in metal oxides, likely resulting from interactions between the CQDs and the NPG substrate. The O(II) peak is linked to hydroxyl groups (–OH) and possibly adsorbed water, which increase the hydrophilicity of the composite, facilitating electrolyte penetration and ion transport. The O(III) peak represents oxygen in carbonyl or carboxyl groups (C = O), commonly found in CQDs. These oxygen-containing groups significantly contribute to the pseudocapacitive behavior of the material, enabling faradaic reactions during charge and discharge cycles. The high-resolution C 1 s spectrum (Fig. 7c) provides insights into the carbon species present in the composite. Three main peaks were observed: C–C at 284.8 eV, C-O-C at 286.2 eV, and O-C = O at 288.5 eV. The C–C peak indicates the presence of sp2-hybridized carbon, commonly associated with graphitic materials like CQDs. This graphitic structure enhances the electrical conductivity of the composite, facilitating faster electron transfer during electrochemical processes. The C-O-C peak corresponds to ether-like carbon–oxygen bonds, likely derived from CQDs, contributing to both the conductivity and functional diversity of the material. The high-resolution N 1 s spectrum (Fig. 7d) displays two prominent peaks: N–H at 400.1 eV and N+ at 401.5 eV. The N–H peak is attributed to nitrogen bonded with hydrogen within the PPy polymer, while the N+ peak represents positively charged nitrogen species, such as protonated amine or quaternary nitrogen, which are characteristic of the conductive nature of PPy. The presence of nitrogen in the PPy structure confirms the retention of the polymer's conductive properties within the composite material.

Fig. 8 a shows a photograph of the CQDs solution glowing under UV light, highlighting the characteristic photoluminescence of CQDs. The observed blue luminescence reflects the presence of surface functional groups and quantum confinement effects within the CQDs, indicating successful synthesis with high-quality luminescent properties. This luminescence suggests thepotential for enhancing electrochemical properties by facilitating effective charge transfer and interaction with the PPy matrix. Fig. 8b presents the CV curves for CQDs-PPy/NPG electrodes prepared at various ultrasonic powers (50 W, 100 W, 150 W, and 200 W) at a scan rate of 50 mV/s. The nearly rectangular shape of the curves across all samples indicates strong capacitive behavior, confirming the pseudocapacitive characteristics of the CQDs-PPy/NPG composite [52]. The CV curve for the 50 W sample showed a lower current response, indicating limited charge storage capability due to reduced surface area and uneven CQD deposition. In comparison, higher power samples exhibited significantly increased current densities, signifying enhanced capacitive performance. Importantly, the 150 W sample displayed the largest enclosed area in the CV curve, indicating optimal charge storage, likely due to uniform CQD dispersion within the PPy matrix and effective interaction with the NPG substrate, both contributing to improved conductivity and capacitance. However, at 200 W, a slight decrease in current density was observed, suggesting that excessive ultrasonic power may lead to CQD overgrowth or aggregation, which could reduce effective surface area and active sites for charge storage.

Fig. 9 a displays the CV curves for CQDs-PPy electrodes at varying scan rates (10, 20, 50, 100, and 200 mV/s). An increase in scan rate resulted in a proportional rise in current response while maintaining the nearly rectangular shape of the curves, which signifies excellent capacitive behavior. However, at the highest scan rate of 200 mV/s, slight distortion was noted in the CV shape, indicating a deviation from ideal capacitive behavior due to increased resistance or diffusion limitations. The largest specific capacitance values were recorded at lower scan rates, as these allowed for more thorough charging and discharging of the electrode [53]. Fig. 9b illustrates the correlation between ultrasonic power and the deposition quality of the CQDs-PPy/NPG composite. As the ultrasonic power rose from 50 W to 200 W, the deposition mass increased substantially, peaking at 0.33 mg at 200 W. This trend indicates that higher ultrasonic power enhanced both the uniformity and thickness of the CQDs-PPy layer on the NPG substrate, contributing to an improved deposition mass. However, as previously analyzed, excessively high ultrasonic power may cause CQD agglomeration, which could adversely affect the overall electrochemical performance.

Fig. 9c displays the GCD curves at 5 A/g for CQDs-PPy/NPG electrodes synthesized at varying ultrasonic powers. The sample prepared at 150 W exhibited the longest discharge time, indicating the highest energy storage capacity among the tested ultrasonic power levels. It also achieved a coulombic efficiency of 96.8 %, the best among all samples, reflecting a well-balanced performance in discharge time and efficiency. The 200 W sample also demonstrated a similar coulombic efficiency of 91.6 %. However, the 50 W sample displayed the shortest discharge time and the lowest coulombic efficiency at 82.1 %, signifying its reduced energy storage capacity. The 100 W sample, with a coulombic efficiency of 86.7 %, outperformed the 50 W sample but remained below the efficiencies of the 150 W and 200 W samples. Fig. 9d illustrates the specific capacitance values of CQDs-PPy/NPG electrodes synthesized at various ultrasonic powers as a function of scan rate. At a low scan rate of 10 mV/s, the 150 W sample achieved the highest specific capacitance of 673.6F/g, followed by the 200 W sample at 621.3F/g. As the scan rate increased, specific capacitance decreased across all samples due to the reduced time available for ion diffusion and interaction with active sites. The 150 W sample consistently displayed improved performance at all scan rates, highlighting both high capacitance and commendable rate capability, with a rate capability of 93.1 %. The 200 W sample followed with a capability of 81.9 %. On the other hand, the 50 W and 100 W samples exhibited lower rate capabilities of 57.3 % and 69.7 %, respectively. These findings indicate that an ultrasonic power of approximately 150 W is optimal for achieving both energy storage and rate performance. Detailed data from the experiment are presented in Table 3 .

In Fig. 10 a, the Nyquist plots for CQDs-PPy/NPG electrodes display a characteristic semicircle in the high-frequency region, indicative of charge transfer resistance (R_ct). Meanwhile, the linear region observed in the low-frequency domain reflects ion diffusion within the electrode material [54]. The small inset presents a magnified view of the Nyquist plot for the 150 W sample, which reveals the smallest semicircle in the high-frequency region, signifying the lowest R_ct. This finding indicates excellent electron conductivity and charge transfer efficiency, likely attributed to the optimal dispersion and integration of CQDs within the PPy/NPG structure. Furthermore, the straight line in the low-frequency region approaches a vertical orientation, suggesting enhanced capacitive behavior and improved ion diffusion within the material. The fitted equivalent circuit model in Fig. 10b illustrates that the electrochemical behavior of the CQDs-PPy/NPG electrode involves both charge storage and ion transport mechanisms. The 150 W sample's lowest R_ct value confirms its improved charge transfer efficiency, consistent with the observations made in the Nyquist plot.

Fig. 11 a presents the cycle stability performance of the CQDs-PPy/NPG composite synthesized at 150 W over 20,000 cycles. The electrode maintained 94.2 % of its initial capacitance, indicating higher long-term stability. This high retention rate reflects the material's exceptional mechanical and electrochemical stability, with minimal degradation of active sites throughout the repeated charge/discharge cycles. In Fig. 11b, the Ragone plot compares the energy and power density of the CQDs-PPy/NPG composite with various reported electrode materials. In Table 4 , the specific capacitance values of various electrode materials are compared across different current densities and electrolytes. The sample synthesized at 150 W shows a higher energy density of 513.7 Wh/kg at a power density of 5 W/kg, outperforming other materials, such as PC@AuNP-1 (421.4 Wh/kg at 0.5 W/kg) [55], HPAuNPs/RGo (351.6 Wh/kg at 0.045 W/kg) [56], PPy@NPCNFs (440.0 Wh/kg at 2 W/kg) [57], BSNC-x (342.1 Wh/kg at 1 W/kg) [58], HHG-PPy (233.5 Wh/kg at 0.5 W/kg) [59], and PPy-NPG (418.9 Wh/kg at 1 W/kg) [60]. This improved performance can be attributed to the synergistic combination of CQDs and PPy. The CQDs enhance conductivity and introduce additional active sites for charge storage, while the NPG substrate provides a large surface area that facilitates ion diffusion. The optimal ultrasonic power of 150 W results in improved dispersion of CQDs within the PPy matrix, significantly contributing to the composite's higher energy storage capacity in comparison to other materials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical analysis of CQDs-PPy/NPG//AC asymmetric supercapacitor
ce:section.ce:para: The electrochemical performance of the CQDs-PPy/NPG//AC asymmetric supercapacitor (ASC) was evaluated through CV, GCD measurements, and energy-power density analysis (Ragone plot). In Fig. 12 a, the CV curves of the AC and CQDs-PPy/NPG electrodes at a scan rate of 50 mV/s are presented. The CV profile of the CQDs-PPy/NPG electrode displayed a quasi-rectangular shape with pronounced redox peaks, indicating a combination of capacitive behavior and faradaic charge storage. However, the AC electrode exhibited a more rectangular shape without significant redox peaks, characteristic of EDLC. The greater current response of the CQDs-PPy/NPG electrode compared to the AC electrode underscores its higher charge storage capacity, attributed to the enhanced pseudocapacitance from the CQDs and PPy integrated within the NPG matrix. The CV curves of the CQDs-PPy/NPG//AC ASC, measured at 50 mV/s across different potential windows, are illustrated in Fig. 12b. As the potential window increased from 0.6 V to 1.5 V, the CV area expanded, indicating enhanced energy storage capacity. At the maximum potential window of 0–1.5 V, the ASC exhibited a nearly symmetric CV curve, reflecting excellent electrochemical stability and reversibility over a broad potential range. The well-defined redox peaks further suggest effective utilization of the CQDs-PPy/NPG electrode within the asymmetric configuration, allowing the ASC to exploit both EDLC from the AC and pseudocapacitance from the CQDs-PPy/NPG. Fig. 12c displays the GCD curves of the CQDs-PPy/NPG//AC ASC at different current densities ranging from 1 A/g to 10 A/g. The ASC maintained its characteristic triangular GCD profile, indicating its ability to sustain high current loads with minimal energy loss. This performance at elevated current densities highlights the high-rate capability of the CQDs-PPy/NPG//AC ASC, making it well-suited for applications that demand rapid energy delivery. In Fig. 12d, the Ragone plot compares the energy and power densities of the CQDs-PPy/NPG//AC ASC with those of other reported materials. The ASC developed in this study achieved a high energy density of 42.8 Wh/kg at a power density of 2000 W/kg, demonstrating improved performance compared to other materials such as BC/Fe3O4@PPy//AC(24.1 Wh/kg, 800 W/kg) [61], PPy@Ti3C2Tx//AC (26.7 Wh/kg, 2000 W/kg) [62], Ov-Co3O4@PPy//AC (39.8 Wh/kg, 600 W/kg) [63], MnO2/g-C3N4@PPy//AC (33.2 Wh/kg, 3000 W/kg) and WS2@PPy//AC (23.4 Wh/kg, 2700 W/kg) [64,65]. At higher power densities, reaching up to 7000 W/kg, the ASC maintained a remarkable energy density of 30.1 Wh/kg, illustrating an effective balance between energy storage and power performance.

This study successfully synthesized CQDs-PPy/NPG composite electrodes using ultrasonic-assisted electrodeposition, with ultrasonic powers varying from 50 W to 200 W. A comprehensive analysis of the microstructure and electrochemical performance demonstrated that ultrasonic power significantly influences the uniformity, dispersion, and integration of CQDs within the PPy matrix. The electrode synthesized at 150 W exhibited the best performance among the samples, achieving a high specific capacitance of 673.6F/g at 5 mV/s, excellent rate capability with 627.1F/g at 100 mV/s, and remarkable cycling stability, retaining 94.2 % of its capacitance over 20,000 cycles. This performance is attributed to the optimal CQDs dispersion, enhanced mass transport, and improved electrode–electrolyte interaction, collectively contributing to higher energy storage and stability. Similarly, the 150 W electrode demonstrated an energy density of 513.7 Wh/kg at a power density of 5 W/kg, underscoring its potential for high-performance supercapacitor applications. These findings indicate that optimizing ultrasonic power during electrodeposition offers an effective approach to enhance composite electrode performance for energy storage devices. Furthermore, the CQDs-PPy/NPG composite was integrated into an ASC, with AC serving as the negative electrode. The ASC achieved an impressive energy density of 42.8 Wh/kg at a power density of 2000 W/kg, highlighting its potential for advanced energy storage applications.

Fafeng Xia: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis.
